preFix.title: Making good after removing ceramic wall tiles
preFix.subtitle: Repair damaged plaster and render after old tiles come off
preFix.excerpt: How to repair a wall after ceramic tiles have been removed: assessing the damage, hacking off loose plaster, re-rendering damaged areas, applying finishing plaster or skim coat, and getting a surface ready for painting or re-tiling.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. British Gypsum White Book. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: When old ceramic tiles come off a wall, they rarely leave the plaster behind. The adhesive either pulls chunks of render with it or leaves a patchwork of adhesive residue and bare masonry. Before a wall can be painted or re-tiled it must be made flat, sound, and plumb again. The repair process follows the same sequence regardless of the substrate: assess, hack out, render or skim, and finish.
A cold chisel and SDS hammer drill for removing old adhesive.

for deeper voids. Finishing plaster (multi-finish or one-coat). A plasterer's hawk and trowel, a float, a large filling knife, and 120-grit sandpaper on a sanding block. Buckets, a mixing paddle, and water.
Step 1, remove old adhesive and loose plaster

Tap across the wall with a knuckle; hollow sounds indicate loose plaster that must come off before any repair material is applied. Hack it off with a bolster chisel and hammer, cutting back to solid render at all edges. Scrape old tile adhesive from any remaining plaster using the scraper; an angle grinder with a sanding disc or an SDS drill with a chisel bit cuts through stubborn adhesive faster.

Brush diluted bonding agent (1 part PVA to 4 parts water) over the bare masonry and any remaining plaster face that will receive new render. Allow to dry until tacky, then apply a second coat of full-strength bonding agent and allow it to reach a sticky tack (not fully dry). This is the window for applying the first plaster coat.
Step 3, apply bonding plaster for deep areas

For areas deeper than 6 mm, apply bonding plaster in layers no more than 12 mm thick. Mix to a stiff creamy consistency and apply with the hawk and trowel, pressing firmly into the substrate and ruling off flat with a straight-edge. Key the surface with a comb scratcher before it fully sets to provide grip for the next coat. Allow 24 hours before overcoating with the finishing plaster.

Apply one-coat or multi-finish finishing plaster over the bonding coat and any existing plaster to a 2 to 3 mm thickness. Skim the trowel over the surface in crossing strokes to level, then polish the surface with a barely damp trowel as the plaster sets. Allow to dry completely (2 to 4 days) before painting.

attrs.fix: Cut back to a clean, slightly undercut edge using a cold chisel, bond the surface, and refill.
attrs.cause: Old plaster edges not properly undercut; the new plaster has nowhere to key.
attrs.symptom: New plaster shrinking away from the edges of old plaster

attrs.fix: Dampen the bonding coat lightly with a brush before applying the finishing coat to slow the set.
attrs.cause: Bonding coat was too dry before the finishing plaster was applied, causing rapid suction.
attrs.symptom: Finishing plaster setting too quickly to work

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
